perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall protect the Disclosing Party’s inter est in the Confidential Information and shall keep the Confidential Information confidential. The Receiving Party shall not disclose, allow access to, transmit or transfer the Confidential Information to any third party without the Disclosing Party’s prior written consent, provided however that the Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information to those of its directors, officers and employees ("Representatives") who have a need to know the Confidential Information for the Purpose provided that the Receiving Party shall ensure that such Representatives treat the Confidential Information as confidential and at all times in a manner consistent with this Agreement and the Receiving Party shall be liable for any loss or damage resulting from any Repre sentative failing to do so.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall endeavor to protect the Disclosing Party’s interest in the Confidential Information and shall keep the Confidential Information confidential to a reasonable extent. The Receiving Party shall not disclose, allow access to, transmit or transfer the Confidential Information to any third party without the Disclosing Party’s prior written consent, provided however that the Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information to those of its directors, officers and employees ("Representatives") who have a need to know the Confidential Information for the Purpose provided that the Receiving Party shall encourage such Representatives to treat the Confidential Information as confidential and the Receiving Party shall only be liable for gross negligence resulting from any Representative failing to do so.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall protect' with 'shall endeavor to protect' and 'shall ensure' with 'shall encourage' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Adding 'to a reasonable extent' introduces subjective interpretation, and raising the liability standard to 'gross negligence' reduces the accountability for breaches. This creates an ambiguous legal obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.7 requires organizations to protect personal information with safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. The modified text's language 'endeavor to protect' and 'to a reasonable extent' create ambiguity, and 'encourage' does not guarantee actual protection. This contradicts the clear obligation to provide appropriate safeguards under PIPEDA, making enforcement uncertain.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: PIPEDA requirements in brief - Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" PIPEDA requirements in brief The Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act ( PIPEDA ) sets the ground rules for how private-sector organizations collect, use, and disclose personal information in the course of for-profit, commercial activities across Canada. PIPEDA also applies to the personal information of employees of federally-regulated businesses. On this page Key responsibilities under PIPEDA The 10 principles Does the Act apply to your business? Provincial privacy laws Information that crosses borders Federally regulated organizations What is personal information? What is not covered by PIPEDA ? Key responsibilities under PIPEDA Businesses that are subject to the Act must follow these 10 fair information principles to protect personal information, which are set out in Schedule 1 of PIPEDA . By following these principles, you will contribute to building trust in your business and in the digital economy. The 10 principles Accountability Identifying purposes Consent Limiting collection Limiting use, disclosure, and retention Accuracy Safeguards Openness Individual access Challenging compliance Read more about how to comply with these 10 principles in your organization. Does the Act apply to your business? PIPEDA applies to private-sector organizations across Canada that collect, use, or disclose personal information in the course of a commercial activity . The law defines a commercial activity as any particular transaction, act, or conduct, or any regular course of conduct that is of a commercial character, including the selling, bartering or leasing of donor, membership or other fundraising lists. Provincial privacy laws Alberta , British Columbia and Quebec have their own private-sector privacy laws that have been deemed substantially similar to PIPEDA . Organizations that are subject to a substantially similar provincial privacy law are generally exempt from PIPEDA with respect to the collection, use, or disclosure of personal information that occurs within that province. Ontario, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia and Newfoundland and Labrador have also adopted substantially similar legislation regarding the collection, use, and disclosure of personal health information. Information that crosses borders All businesses that operate in Canada and handle personal information that crosses provincial or national borders in the course of commercial activities are subject to PIPEDA , regardless of the province or territory in which they are based (including provinces with substantially similar legislation). Federally regulated organizations Federally regulated organizations that conduct business in Canada are always subject to PIPEDA . The Act also applies to their employees’ personal information. These organizations include: airports, aircraft and airlines banks and authorized foreign banks inter-provincial or international transportation companies telecommunications companies offshore drilling operations radio and television broadcasters Note: Organizations in the Northwest Territories, Yukon, and Nunavut are considered federally regulated, and are therefore also covered by PIPEDA . If you are not sure if your business is subject to PIPEDA , please consult find the right organization to contact about a privacy issue . What is personal information? Under PIPEDA , personal information includes any factual or subjective information, recorded or not, about an identifiable individual. This includes information in any form, such as: age, name, ID numbers, income, ethnic origin, or blood type opinions, evaluations, comments, social status, or disciplinary actions employee files, credit records, loan records, medical records, existence of a dispute between a consumer and a merchant, intentions (for example, to acquire goods or services, or change jobs) What is not covered by PIPEDA ? There are some i
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially contradicting PIPEDA's requirement for appropriate safeguards. The original text uses "shall protect" and "shall ensure," establishing clear obligations. The changed text introduces ambiguity with "endeavor to protect," "to a reasonable extent," and "encourage," and raises the liability standard to 'gross negligence'. According to the Law Explanation, this weakens the obligation to protect, conflicting with the intent of PIPEDA. The scraped snippet 2 states PIPEDA sets the ground rules for how private-sector organizations collect, use and disclose personal information. By weakening the language around 'protecting' and 'ensuring' the protection of confidential information, the obligation is made ambiguous, therefore creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party is required to disclose Confidential Information pursuant to any applicable law or an order from a court of competent jurisdiction, the Receiving Party shall only disclose such portion of the Confidential Information that it is legally required to disclose , and shall use all reasonable efforts to obtain confidential treatment for any Confidential Information so disclosed. The Receiving Party shall promptly notify the Disclosing Party of the required disclosure and any relevant information in respect thereto so that the Disclosing Party may take appropriate steps to protect such Confidential Information from such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party believes it is required to disclose Confidential Information pursuant to any applicable law or what appears to be an order from a court of competent jurisdiction, the Receiving Party may disclose any portion of the Confidential Information that it deems necessary to disclose, and may attempt to obtain confidential treatment for any Confidential Information so disclosed. The Receiving Party will endeavor to notify the Disclosing Party of the potential disclosure and any information it considers relevant so that the Disclosing Party might attempt to take steps to protect such Confidential Information from such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is required' to 'believes it is required' and 'may disclose any portion that it deems necessary' introduces significant ambiguity, allowing the Receiving Party subjective discretion. Further weakening the language by substituting 'shall use all reasonable efforts' with 'may attempt to obtain' and 'shall promptly notify' with 'will endeavor to notify' reduces the obligation to protect confidential information. This introduces multiple points of subjective interpretation, making the obligation difficult to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.7 necessitates implementing measures to protect information. The ambiguous phrases introduced undermine this principle by making the required disclosures and protective measures discretionary. By not requiring strict adherence to legally mandated disclosure requirements and weakening the effort to seek confidential treatment, the modification creates a conflict with PIPEDA's data protection mandates.

There are some i
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates specific actions regarding confidential information disclosure, aligning with the principle of implementing safeguards to protect information. The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The modification from 'is required' to 'believes it is required' and the shift from 'shall' to 'may' significantly reduces the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions, undermining PIPEDA's data protection mandates as explained by the LLM. The scraped snippet states that businesses subject to PIPEDA must follow the 10 fair information principles to protect personal information. This change introduces ambiguity in the duty to protect therefore contradicting those principles.

perturbation.original_text: The Confidential Information shall not be copied or reproduced in any form or stored in a retrieval system or database by the Receiv ing Party without the prior consent of the Disclosing Party except for such copies and storage as may reasonably be required internally by the Receiving Party for the Purpose. All copies of the Confidential Information shall contain the same proprietary n otices which may appear on the original.
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidential Information should generally not be copied or reproduced extensively in any form or stored indefinitely in a retrieval system or database by the Receiving Party without occasionally seeking the consent of the Disclosing Party except for copies and storage that the Receiving Party considers reasonably required internally for the Purpose. Copies of the Confidential Information may contain proprietary notices, if conveniently available.
perturbation.explanation: Weakening 'shall not' to 'should generally not' makes compliance non-mandatory. Adding 'extensively' and 'indefinitely' creates vague limits on copying and storage. 'Occasionally seeking the consent' reduces the requirement for prior consent. Replacing 'shall contain' with 'may contain, if conveniently available' reduces the obligation to include proprietary notices. These changes create a high degree of ambiguity and reduce accountability.
perturbation.law_citation: Copyright Act, R.S.C., 1985, c. C-42, s. 27
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 27 of the Canadian Copyright Act addresses copyright infringement. The original clause aimed to protect the Disclosing Party's copyright by preventing unauthorized copying and ensuring proprietary notices are maintained. The modification creates ambiguity regarding the extent to which copying is permissible, the duration of storage, the need for consent, and the inclusion of copyright notices. This contradicts the protections afforded under copyright law by weakening the prohibitions against unauthorized reproduction and distribution.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ork as a result of the use of that service. Marginal note: Factors (2.4) Â In determining whether a person has infringed copyright under subsection (2.3), the court may consider (a) Â whether the person expressly or implicitly marketed or promoted the service as one that could be used to enable acts of copyright infringement; (b) Â whether the person had knowledge that the service was used to enable a significant number of acts of copyright infringement; (c) Â whether the service has significant uses other than to enable acts of copyright infringement; (d) Â the personâs ability, as part of providing the service, to limit acts of copyright infringement, and any action taken by the person to do so; (e) Â any benefits the person received as a result of enabling the acts of copyright infringement; and (f) Â the economic viability of the provision of the service if it were not used to enable acts of copyright infringement. Marginal note: Knowledge of importer (3) Â In determining whether there is an infringement under subsection (2) in the case of an activity referred to in any of paragraphs (2)(a) to (d) in relation to a copy that was imported in the circumstances referred to in paragraph (2)(e), it is irrelevant whether the importer knew or should have known that the importation of the copy infringed copyright. Marginal note: Plates (4) Â It is an infringement of copyright for any person to make or possess a plate that has been specifically designed or adapted for the purpose of making infringing copies of a work or other subject-matter. Marginal note: Public performance for profit (5) Â It is an infringement of copyright for any person, for profit, to permit a theatre or other place of entertainment to be used for the performance in public of a work or other subject-matter without the consent of the owner of the copyright unless that person was not aware, and had no reasonable ground for suspecting, that the performance would be an infringement of copyright. R.S., 1985, c. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the copyright protections intended in the original contract. The change from 'shall not' to 'should generally not' and the added qualifiers like 'extensively' and 'indefinitely' introduce discretion and make enforcement difficult. The shift from 'shall contain' to 'may contain, if conveniently available' further undermines the obligation to include proprietary notices. The law explanation states that Section 27 of the Canadian Copyright Act addresses copyright infringement, and the modifications weaken prohibitions against unauthorized reproduction and distribution. The provided snippet of the Canadian Copyright Act discusses copyright infringement. By introducing ambiguity, the modified text creates an environment where infringement is more likely and harder to prevent, directly contradicting the intent to protect copyright.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall upon request immediately return to the Disclosing Party or destroy, as directed by the Disclosing Party , the Confidential Information and all copies thereof in any form whatsoever under the power or control of the Receiving Party or its Representatives and immediately destroy all compilations, analysis or derivative work relating to the Confidential Information. Where the Receiving Party satisfies its obligation to return or destroy the embodiments of the Confidential Information by destroying such embodiments, the Receiving Party will deliver to the Disclosing Party a certificate signed by an officer of the Receiving Party certifying such destruction of such embodiments .
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall upon reasonable request return to the Disclosing Party or destroy, as reasonably directed by the Disclosing Party, most of the Confidential Information and tangible copies thereof under the direct control of the Receiving Party. The Receiving Party will try to destroy compilations, analysis or derivative work. The Receiving Party will endeavor to provide a certificate confirming destruction, if feasible.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'upon reasonable request' replaces 'upon request immediately,' making the return/destruction timeline subjective. Limiting the scope to 'most of the Confidential Information and tangible copies thereof' excludes potentially relevant electronic data. 'Direct control' narrows the scope of responsibility. 'Will try to destroy' weakens the obligation to destroy derivative works. 'Will endeavor to provide, if feasible' weakens the certificate requirement. These changes introduce ambiguity and loopholes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA, Principle 4.5 requires organizations to destroy or erase personal information that is no longer required to fulfill the purpose for which it was collected. The modified clause introduces ambiguity by making the return/destruction timeline subjective and excluding certain data from the destruction requirement. This contradicts the requirement for complete and timely destruction under PIPEDA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities regarding the destruction of confidential information, potentially contradicting PIPEDA's requirement for complete and timely destruction. While the scraped snippet 2 does not directly specify the 'complete and timely' aspect, the Law Explanation highlights that Principle 4.5 of PIPEDA requires organizations to destroy or erase personal information that is no longer required. The modified clause uses terms like 'reasonable request,' 'most of the Confidential Information,' 'direct control,' 'will try to destroy,' and 'if feasible,' which weaken the obligation to destroy information, opening the door to non-compliance. The original text was more stringent in requiring immediate action and complete destruction. The ambiguity introduced by the changed text undermines the clarity and enforceability of the destruction requirement under PIPEDA.
